hook: The oldest stone house in Paris, built in 1407 by the bookseller-scribe whom legend turned into an immortal alchemist.
facts: The facade carries a frieze inscribed with the completion date 1407, making it the oldest surviving stone-built house in Paris.
It was built by Nicolas Flamel, a 14th-15th-century Parisian manuscript-seller (libraire-juré) and copyist, with his wife Pernelle.
Flamel endowed it as an almshouse: the ground floor was let to labourers who, in lieu of high rent, were asked to recite prayers for the dead, a request carved into the stone facade.
The building was classified a monument historique in 1911.
The ground floor today houses the Auberge Nicolas Flamel restaurant.
legend: Centuries after his death (1418), Flamel acquired a posthumous reputation as an alchemist who decoded a mysterious book and discovered the Philosopher's Stone, transmuting lead into gold; this 17th-century legend, not contemporary fact, explains his wealth as alchemical.
Some occult tales claim Flamel and Pernelle never truly died but achieved immortality; he later appears as a character in Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone.

sources.note: 1407 date inscribed on frieze, oldest stone house, almshouse with prayer inscription, classified 1911, coordinates 48.86361/2.35306
51 rue de Montmorency, oldest stone house, Flamel as bookseller and alchemy legend
Official Paris tourism: built 1407, charitable purpose, prayer-for-rent inscription

w: The oldest stone house in Paris, its 1407 date carved into the facade along with a strange request: the labourers who lodged here paid their rent in prayers for the dead. Flamel sold manuscripts for a living; only centuries later did legend recast him as the immortal alchemist who made the Philosopher's Stone.

hook: Ledoux's monumental 1788 customs rotunda, a survivor of the hated tax wall that helped spark the Revolution, now facing the Bassin de la Villette.
facts: Built 1784-1788 by the architect Claude-Nicolas Ledoux as one of the barrieres (tollhouses) of the Wall of the Farmers-General, the ring built to levy octroi duty on goods entering Paris.
Its design pairs a square ground floor of arcades with a circular drum above, using bold simplified geometry, coupled Doric columns and Roman arches typical of Ledoux's visionary neoclassicism.
The tax wall it served was deeply resented and was among the grievances feeding popular anger before 1789.
After the Revolution it was reused as a salt store and later as a municipal-guard barracks.
It was classified a monument historique in 1907 and stands beside the Bassin de la Villette.

sources.note: Ledoux, 1784-1788, barriere of the Farmers-General wall, monument historique, coordinates 48.88333/2.36944
Place Stalingrad location, octroi tollhouse, salt store and barracks reuse, faces Bassin de la Villette

w: Ledoux's monumental customs rotunda of 1788, a survivor of the despised tax wall whose tollgates helped stoke the Revolution. Coupled Doric columns and a great round drum over an arcaded base -- the architect's visionary geometry at full pitch. Later a salt store, then a barracks; now it faces the Bassin de la Villette.

hook: The world's first elevated park, laid along an old railway viaduct in 1993 and the acknowledged inspiration for New York's High Line.
facts: A 4.7 km linear park opened in 1993 on the disused Vincennes railway line, running from Bastille toward the Bois de Vincennes.
Its western stretch sits about 10 m above the street atop the Viaduc des Arts, a row of brick arches whose vaults now hold craft workshops and showrooms.
It predates New York's High Line (opened 2009) and is widely cited as that project's model.
It is named after the agronomist and environmentalist Rene Dumont.
The viaduct was saved from demolition when the City of Paris intervened in the early 1980s.

sources.note: 4.7 km, opened 1993, Vincennes line, Viaduc des Arts ~10 m elevation, named for Rene Dumont
Promenade Plantee as the original / inspiration for the High Line
Viaduc des Arts runs 1-129 Avenue Daumesnil from Place de la Bastille; corroborates western/Bastille start coordinate near Rue de Lyon (~48.850/2.370)

w: The world's first elevated park, run along a disused railway viaduct from Bastille in 1993 -- the model New York openly copied for its High Line sixteen years later. Its western stretch rides ten metres up on brick arches, the vaults below now craft workshops. Saved from demolition by the city, named for an agronomist.

hook: An 1828 iron-and-glass covered passage that became Paris's 'Little India', dense with South Asian groceries and curry houses.
facts: Opened 15 April 1828, one of the early Parisian covered passages and among the first to use iron and glass.
Cutting the Boulevard de Strasbourg around 1854 sliced the passage in two; the western section keeps its glass roof, the eastern part is open to the sky.
It never became a luxury arcade, instead housing second-hand clothes shops, reading rooms and public baths in the 19th century.
Its transformation began in 1973 when Antoine Ponnoussamy opened the first Indian shop, drawing low-rent South Asian businesses thereafter.
It is now known as 'Little India', lined with Indian, Pakistani and Bangladeshi restaurants and shops.

sources.note: Opened 15 April 1828, split by Boulevard de Strasbourg, coordinates 48.87112/2.35528
Little India history, 1973 first Indian shop, iron-and-glass, 46 rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis

w: An 1828 iron-and-glass arcade that never went luxury -- second-hand clothes and public baths once, then, from 1973, a first Indian shop, and after it a whole 'Little India' of curry houses and grocers. A boulevard cut through in the 1850s sliced it in two. Steam, spice and strip light under the old glass roof.

hook: A serene 1607 brick-and-stone plague hospital whose courtyard is a near-twin of the Place des Vosges, hidden behind a working hospital.
facts: Founded by edict of Henri IV in May 1607 (first stone laid July 1607) to isolate plague victims outside the city walls, completed around 1611-1612.
Its brick-and-stone architecture, attributed to Claude Vellefaux with Claude Chastillon, deliberately echoes the contemporary Place des Vosges (Place Royale).
Four wings, each about 120 m long, enclose a large rectangular courtyard, originally ringed by a buffer zone to keep the contagious apart from the city.
It became a pioneering centre of dermatology in the 19th century and remains a functioning AP-HP hospital.
The historic quadrangle survives near the Canal Saint-Martin while modern hospital buildings stand alongside.

sources.note: Founded 1607 by Henri IV, plague isolation hospital, Vellefaux/Chastillon, 120 m wings, coordinates 48.87472/2.36833
1607-1612 construction, brick-and-stone echoing Place des Vosges, four wings around courtyard

w: Behind a working hospital hides a serene 1607 courtyard of brick and stone, a near-twin of the Place des Vosges built the same decade. Henri IV raised it to isolate plague victims outside the walls; four 120-metre wings enclose the quadrangle, once ringed by a quarantine buffer. Dermatology was half-invented in these wards.

hook: A short Belleville lane whose walls are an ever-repainted open-air gallery, the heart of eastern Paris street art.
facts: A roughly 150 m street in Belleville (20e), the best-known street-art spot of the arrondissement.
It first appears on cadastral maps in 1812, when Belleville was a separate commune annexed to Paris only in 1860.
Its name comes from the Denoyez family, who ran a popular guinguette dance hall ('Folie Denoyez') here in the 1830s.
Belleville's industrial wasteland and blank walls made it fertile ground for graffiti as hip-hop culture arrived in the 1980s; the street became a street-art hub from the 2000s.
The works are constantly painted over and renewed, so the walls change continually.

sources.note: Belleville street-art street, official Paris region tourism listing
~150 m, 1812 cadastre, Denoyez family / Folie Denoyez guinguette, coordinates 48.871467/2.378596

w: A short Belleville lane whose walls are repainted so often they are never the same twice -- the open-air heart of eastern Paris street art. It carried the name of the Denoyez family, who ran a riotous guinguette here in the 1830s, long before the spray cans. Blank industrial walls and cheap rents drew the writers in the 1980s.

hook: A bronze of a journalist shot dead in 1870, sculpted lying as he fell, now rubbed shiny in three places by visitors chasing a fertility legend.
facts: Victor Noir (born Yvan Salmon) was a 21-year-old journalist shot dead by Pierre-Napoleon Bonaparte on 10 January 1870, becoming a Republican martyr.
The life-size bronze recumbent statue was made by the sculptor Jules Dalou (completed around 1890), depicting Noir at the instant he fell backward, top hat at his side.
His body was transferred to Pere-Lachaise (division 92) in 1891.
The bronze's green patina has been worn to a polished shine in three areas, the lips, the feet and a pronounced bulge at the trouser front, from decades of touching.
legend: Popular superstition holds that rubbing the figure's groin (and kissing its lips, touching its feet) brings fertility, virility or a future spouse; visitors leave flowers and sometimes babies' shoes in thanks. This is modern folklore, not anything connected to Noir's life.

sources.note: Dalou recumbent bronze, division 92, fertility legend, worn shiny areas
Division 92 location confirmation; coordinate ~48.8608/2.3966 along Avenue Transversale No. 2
Fertility legend, shot 1870, statue worn shiny

w: A young journalist shot dead in 1870, sculpted by Dalou lying exactly as he fell, top hat at his side -- a Republican martyr in bronze. Three patches gleam gold against the green patina: the lips, the feet, and a bulge at the trousers, all rubbed by visitors. The fertility legend is pure modern invention, nothing to do with Noir.

hook: Paris's centre for architecture and urbanism, housed in a soaring 1879 cast-iron hall built as a private 'people's museum'.
facts: Located at 21 boulevard Morland in the 4th arrondissement, near the former Celestine arsenal that gives it its name.
The cast-iron-and-glass building was erected in 1878-1879 for the timber merchant and amateur painter Laurent-Louis Borniche, who wanted a free 'popular museum' for his roughly 2,000 paintings.
After Borniche died (1883) the building passed through commercial uses and was bought by the La Samaritaine department store in 1922.
Rehabilitated by architects Bernard Reichen and Philippe Robert, it reopened in 1988 as the city's free centre for architecture, urban planning and exhibitions.
Its permanent display includes a large illuminated scale model of Paris.

sources.note: 21 bd Morland, built 1878-1879 for Borniche as popular museum, reopened 1988 as architecture centre, coordinates 48.85056/2.36222
Official site: building history, Borniche, cast-iron hall, current architecture-and-urbanism mission

w: Paris's free centre for architecture and city-planning, under the glass roof of an 1879 cast-iron hall -- built by a timber merchant who wanted a 'people's museum' for his two thousand paintings. After his death it served the Samaritaine, then reopened to the public in 1988. Inside: a vast illuminated scale model of the whole city.

hook: A 1st–2nd-century Gallo-Roman amphitheatre that once held 15,000 spectators, now a free public square where Parisians play pétanque among the ancient tiers.
facts: One of the largest amphitheatres built by the Romans in Gaul, dating to the 1st–2nd century AD, when Paris was the settlement of Lutetia.
It served as both a theatre stage and an arena for gladiatorial and animal combats, with tiered seating cut into the hillside.
It was largely buried after the 3rd-century barbarian invasions, when its stone was reused; it was rediscovered in 1869 during the cutting of rue Monge.
A public subscription championed by figures including Victor Hugo saved it from demolition; it was restored to roughly its present state by 1917.
Entry is free, with access via a passage at 47 rue Monge and from rue de Navarre and the Square Capitan.
w: A Gallo-Roman amphitheatre that once seated fifteen thousand, lost for centuries until rue Monge cut through it in 1869. Victor Hugo led the campaign that saved its tiers from demolition. Today there is no ticket booth, just Parisians playing petanque on the arena floor where gladiators once fought. Two thousand years, used as a square.

hook: A taxidermy and natural-history emporium founded in 1831, where stuffed lions, polar bears and drawers of pinned beetles fill an 18th-century mansion above a courtyard.
facts: Founded in 1831 by Jean-Baptiste Deyrolle and built up by his descendants into one of the best-known taxidermy and entomology firms in Paris.
It has occupied 46 rue du Bac since the 1880s, in a townhouse originally built in 1697–1699 and remodelled in 1739 for the son of Louis XIV's banker Samuel Bernard.
The cabinet of curiosities upstairs is open to the public and sells and displays mounted animals, insects, shells and minerals.
A fire in February 2008 destroyed much of the collection; the shop was restocked and reopened.
It inspired Surrealists André Breton and Salvador Dalí and has drawn later artists such as Damien Hirst.
w: Up a staircase on rue du Bac, in a mansion from the 1690s, waits a taxidermy emporium founded in 1831: stuffed lions and polar bears, drawers of pinned beetles, shelves of shells and minerals. A 2008 fire gutted the collection; they restocked and reopened. Breton and Dali loved it, and Damien Hirst after them.

hook: Propped up on a concrete crutch beside the city's oldest church garden stands the oldest tree in Paris, a false acacia planted in 1601.
facts: The tree is a Robinia pseudoacacia (false acacia) sown around 1601 by royal botanist Jean Robin, who introduced the North American species to Europe.
Carl Linnaeus named the genus Robinia in Robin's honour, and the tree's common French name 'robinier' derives from him.
It stands in the Square René-Viviani, beside the medieval church of Saint-Julien-le-Pauvre, near the Seine across from Notre-Dame.
Now around 15 metres tall and leaning heavily, it is supported by a concrete buttress and is classified as a remarkable tree.
It is generally cited as the oldest living tree in Paris.
w: Propped on a concrete crutch beside the city's oldest church garden leans the oldest tree in Paris -- a false acacia sown around 1601 by the royal botanist Jean Robin, who first brought the species from America. Linnaeus named the whole genus Robinia after him. Fifteen metres tall and tilting badly, still in leaf after four centuries.

hook: A genuine Japanese-style pagoda shipped to Paris in the 1890s as a society ballroom, later one of the city's most beloved art-house cinemas.
facts: It was commissioned in the 1890s by François-Émile Morin, a director of the Bon Marché department store, at the height of the craze for Japonisme.
Architect Alexandre Marcel designed the pagoda, completed around 1896, decorated with Japanese-style carvings and panels in the gardens of 57 bis rue de Babylone.
It opened as a cinema in 1931 and became a temple of art-house and Nouvelle Vague film, screening Buñuel, Renoir, Cocteau, Truffaut and Rohmer.
Jean Cocteau's 1959 film 'Le Testament d'Orphée' premiered there.
Listed as a monument historique in 1990, it closed in 2015 and was acquired in 2017 by US real-estate magnate Charles S. Cohen with plans to restore it.
w: A genuine Japanese pagoda, built in the 1890s as a Bon Marche director's ballroom at the height of the Japonisme craze. From 1931 it ran as an art-house cinema and a temple of the Nouvelle Vague -- Bunuel, Cocteau, Truffaut, Rohmer. Cocteau premiered his last film here in 1959. Listed, then shuttered in 2015, awaiting its restorers.

hook: A 1924 brick-vaulted municipal pool in the village-like Butte-aux-Cailles, fed by a deep artesian well that keeps its outdoor water warm year-round.
facts: Designed by architect Louis Bonnier and built 1922–1924 using Hennebique reinforced concrete, with a striking vaulted indoor hall and red-brick facade.
It is supplied by an artesian well drilled in 1893 reaching roughly 580 metres down, drawing naturally warm water at about 28°C.
The complex has three basins: a 33-metre indoor pool and two outdoor pools (25 m and a smaller one).
It was classified as a monument historique in 1990 and marked its centenary in 2024.
It sits on Place Paul Verlaine in the hilltop Butte-aux-Cailles, a former working-class quarter that escaped Haussmann's redevelopment.
w: A 1924 municipal pool of red brick and bold concrete vaults, fed by an artesian well drilled half a kilometre down in 1893 -- its water comes up naturally warm, around 28 degrees. Three basins, one indoor under the great vault, two open to the sky. It crowns a hilltop village that Haussmann's bulldozers somehow missed.

hook: Hidden at the back of a Left Bank courtyard, this 1932 'House of Glass' built of translucent glass blocks is a landmark of modern architecture, slotted beneath a tenant who refused to leave.
facts: Built 1928–1932 at 31 rue Saint-Guillaume by designer Pierre Chareau with architect Bernard Bijvoet and metalworker Louis Dalbet.
Its street and courtyard facades are walls of translucent glass blocks, with exposed steel beams and movable metal screens inside.
An elderly tenant on the top floor of the existing 18th-century building refused to move, so the new house was inserted into the lower floors beneath her, leaving the upper storeys in place.
It was commissioned as a home and gynaecology practice for Dr Jean Dalsace and his wife Annie.
It is a privately owned residence and can only be visited by appointment.
w: At the back of a Left Bank courtyard stands a house with walls of translucent glass block -- Pierre Chareau's 1932 'House of Glass', a landmark of modern design. An elderly tenant on the top floor refused to budge, so the architects simply slid the new house in beneath her, leaving her storey untouched. By appointment only.

hook: Born of the Revolution in 1794 partly from animals seized at Versailles, this is one of the oldest zoos in the world, a maze of 19th-century pavilions in a botanical garden.
facts: Opened on 11 December 1794, it is among the oldest surviving zoos in the world, after Vienna's Schönbrunn (1752).
It was founded largely with animals from the abandoned royal menagerie at Versailles plus beasts from confiscated travelling shows.
It is part of the Muséum national d'Histoire naturelle, within the Jardin des Plantes by the Seine.
Its most famous early resident was Zarafa, the first giraffe in France, who arrived in 1827 after being walked from Marseille to Paris.
It now focuses on small and endangered species and conservation breeding rather than large animals.
w: One of the oldest zoos on earth, opened in 1794 and stocked partly with beasts seized from the royal menagerie at Versailles and from confiscated travelling shows. Its most famous early arrival, Zarafa the giraffe, was walked all the way from Marseille in 1827. A maze of little 19th-century pavilions in a botanical garden by the Seine.

hook: A triangular pocket of flower-named cobbled lanes and low cottages built on a former flood meadow, an unexpected country village hidden among the tower blocks of the 13th.
facts: The Cité Florale was built around 1928 on a triangular plot of former marshy meadow that frequently flooded from the Bièvre river, covered over in 1912.
Because the ground was unstable and flood-prone, only small low houses were permitted, giving it its village scale.
Its streets are named after flowers, including rue des Glycines, rue des Iris, rue des Orchidées, rue des Liserons and the Square des Mimosas.
The houses are draped in wisteria and climbing plants, with Art Deco ironwork, in sharp contrast to the surrounding modern buildings.
It lies in the southwest corner of the 13th arrondissement near the Butte-aux-Cailles.
w: A triangular pocket of cobbled lanes and low cottages, all named for flowers -- rue des Glycines, rue des Iris, des Orchidees -- draped in wisteria and hidden among the tower blocks of the 13th. Built around 1928 on a former Bievre flood-meadow so soft that only small houses were allowed. A country village that the city grew up around.

hook: Paris's last working vineyard clings to the north slope of the Butte, harvested each October in a costumed festival older than most living Parisians.
facts: Roughly 2,000 vines (mainly Pinot Noir and Gamay) grow on the slope between rue des Saules and rue Saint-Vincent, yielding about 1,000-2,000 bottles of 'Cuvée du Clos Montmartre' a year.
The vineyard was planted in 1933 by the Republique libre de Montmartre to block a real-estate development on the slope.
The Fete des Vendanges de Montmartre harvest festival has run since 1934 and falls on the second weekend of October.
Proceeds from the wine auction support social programmes in the 18th arrondissement.
The plot sits directly opposite the Au Lapin Agile cabaret and below the Musee de Montmartre.
legend: Local lore holds that the steep, north-facing slope makes the wine famously difficult, with the cuvee jokingly said to 'make you jump like a goat and dance like a flea'.
w: Paris's last working vineyard, two thousand vines on the steep north slope of the Butte, harvested every October in a costumed festival running since 1934. It was planted in 1933 to block a developer from the hillside; the wine, sold for charity, is famously difficult. The locals joke it 'makes you jump like a goat and dance like a flea'.

hook: A 40-square-metre wall of blue lava tiles in a pocket garden off Place des Abbesses says 'I love you' 311 times in 250 languages.
facts: The wall is made of 612 tiles of enamelled lava and measures about 40 square metres, in Square Jehan-Rictus near Place des Abbesses.
It was created in 2000 by calligrapher Frederic Baron and mural artist Claire Kito.
Baron spent years collecting handwritten 'I love you' phrases from speakers of different languages before the wall was assembled.
Scattered red fragments across the dark tiles are meant to represent the pieces of a broken heart.
w: In a pocket garden off Place des Abbesses, a 40-square-metre wall of dark blue lava tiles says 'I love you' 311 times, in 250 languages. The calligrapher Frederic Baron spent years collecting the phrases by hand before it went up in 2000. Scattered red shards across the tiles stand for the pieces of a broken heart.

hook: The shabby pink cottage cabaret where Picasso, Apollinaire and Modigliani drank still stages chansons nightly, much as it did when it was called the Cabaret of the Assassins.
facts: The cabaret sits at 22 rue des Saules, at the corner of rue Saint-Vincent, beside the Clos Montmartre vineyard.
It was renamed 'Le Lapin Agile' after André Gill painted a sign of a rabbit leaping from a saucepan in the 1870s; the name puns on 'Le lapin a Gill' (Gill's rabbit).
Under Frederic 'Frede' Gerard from 1903 it became a gathering place for Picasso, Apollinaire, Modigliani, Max Jacob, Suzanne Valadon and Utrillo.
Picasso's 1905 painting 'Au Lapin Agile' hung in the cabaret until Frede sold it around 1912 for a pittance; it later sold at Sotheby's in 1989 for about 40.7 million dollars and is now at the Met.
Frede's donkey Lolo was tied to a paintbrush in the 1910 'Lolo' hoax that satirised the Paris avant-garde.
w: The shabby pink cottage cabaret where Picasso, Apollinaire and Modigliani drank, still putting on chansons most nights. It took its name from a painter's sign of a rabbit leaping from a saucepan -- 'le lapin a Gill', Gill's rabbit. Picasso's painting of the place hung here until Frede sold it for a pittance; it later fetched 40 million.

hook: A bronze man bursts halfway out of a stone wall on a quiet square, frozen forever at the moment his magic gift to walk through walls deserted him.
facts: The 2.3-metre bronze sculpture was conceived and made by actor and sculptor Jean Marais and inaugurated on 25 February 1989.
It stands on Place Marcel-Ayme, off rue Norvins, which has borne the writer's name since December 1986.
It depicts Dutilleul, hero of Marcel Aymé's 1943 short story 'Le Passe-Muraille', a man who could walk through walls until he became stuck in one.
Marcel Aymé (1902-1967) lived nearby on the Butte, which is the story's setting.
w: A bronze man bursts halfway out of a stone wall on a quiet Montmartre square, frozen at the instant his power to walk through walls failed him. The actor Jean Marais sculpted it in 1989 for Marcel Ayme's 1943 tale, set on this very Butte where the writer lived. The locals shake his protruding hand. Stuck, mid-escape, forever.

hook: Behind a modest facade on Place Émile-Goudeau stood the ramshackle warren of studios where Picasso painted the first Cubist canvas and modern art was effectively born.
facts: The complex of cheap wood-and-plaster studios stood at 13 rue Ravignan, on Place Emile-Goudeau in Montmartre.
Picasso rented a studio here from 1904 and painted 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' here around 1907.
Residents and regulars included Picasso, Juan Gris, Max Jacob, Kees van Dongen, Amedeo Modigliani and Guillaume Apollinaire.
A fire on 12 May 1970 gutted the building, leaving only the facade; it was rebuilt with studios for artists by 1978.
The name 'Bateau-Lavoir' ('laundry boat') is said to come from its resemblance to the wooden wash-boats moored on the Seine.
w: Behind a modest facade on Place Emile-Goudeau stood a ramshackle warren of cheap studios where, around 1907, Picasso painted 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' and Cubism was effectively born. Gris, Modigliani, Max Jacob and Apollinaire passed through. A 1970 fire left only the front wall; it was rebuilt for artists.

hook: A Baltard-style iron-and-glass market hall at the foot of the Butte is now France's leading shrine to art brut, naive art and the gloriously untrained.
facts: The museum occupies a covered market hall built in 1868 in the iron-and-glass Baltard style, at 2 rue Ronsard below the Sacre-Coeur gardens.
It was founded as a museum in 1986 by publisher Max Fourny and specialises in art brut, naive art, outsider and singular art.
Exhibitions are long-running, typically lasting six months to a year.
The site combines a museum, gallery, bookshop, auditorium and cafe.
w: A Baltard-style iron-and-glass market hall of 1868 at the foot of the Butte, now France's foremost shrine to art brut -- the raw, untaught, gloriously outsider art the academies ignored. Founded as a museum in 1986, it runs long shows of six months or more, with a gallery, bookshop and cafe under the old iron frame.

hook: A mansion frozen in 1914 on the edge of Parc Monceau, left intact by a grieving banker whose family the Holocaust then erased entirely.
facts: The Hotel Moise de Camondo at 63 rue de Monceau was built in 1911-1914 by architect Rene Sergent, modelled on the Petit Trianon at Versailles.
Count Moise de Camondo built it to display his collection of mid-18th-century French furniture, Sevres porcelain and decorative arts.
He bequeathed the house and collection to Les Arts Decoratifs in memory of his son Nissim, an aviator killed in action in 1917; it opened to the public in 1936.
Moise's daughter Beatrice, her husband and their children were deported and murdered at Auschwitz, ending the Camondo line.
w: A mansion frozen in 1914 on the rim of Parc Monceau, modelled on the Petit Trianon and crammed with 18th-century French furniture and Sevres. The banker Moise de Camondo left it to the nation for his son Nissim, an aviator killed in 1917. His daughter and her family were later murdered at Auschwitz -- the whole line erased.

hook: A blazing-red Chinese pagoda erupts out of a sober Haussmannian street, the legacy of an Asian-art dealer who turned a 19th-century mansion inside out.
facts: The pagoda stands at 48 rue de Courcelles in the 8th arrondissement, in the Haussmannian Plaine Monceau quarter.
Chinese art dealer Ching Tsai Loo, who arrived in Paris in 1902, bought an 1880s mansion and had architect Fernand Bloch transform it.
The 1925-1926 conversion raised the building two storeys, added curved pagoda eaves and painted the whole facade bright red.
It still operates as a private gallery and event space, today known as Pagoda Paris / Maison de Loo, with the gallery CTLoo & Cie.
w: A blazing-red Chinese pagoda erupts out of a sober Haussmannian street in the 8th. The Asian-art dealer Ching Tsai Loo bought an 1880s mansion and turned it inside out in 1925 -- two floors added, eaves curved up, the whole front painted vermilion. It trades still as a gallery of Asian art. The most startling thing on rue de Courcelles.

hook: A gilded replica of the Statue of Liberty's torch, never meant as a memorial, became the world's spontaneous shrine to Princess Diana above the tunnel where she died.
facts: The monument is a full-size gold-leaf replica of the flame from the Statue of Liberty's torch, about 3.5 metres tall on a marble pedestal.
It was given to France in 1989 by the International Herald Tribune to mark its centenary and Franco-American friendship.
It stands at the north end of the Pont de l'Alma, directly above the road tunnel where Diana, Princess of Wales, was fatally injured in August 1997.
Mourners turned it into an unofficial Diana shrine, covering the base with tributes; the square was officially named Place Diana in 2019.
w: A full-size gold replica of the Statue of Liberty's torch, given to Paris in 1989 for a newspaper's centenary -- and never meant as a memorial. But it stands directly above the Alma tunnel where Diana died in 1997, and mourners turned it into her shrine, heaping the base with tributes. The square took her name in 2019.
